summary: Mozart's 1787 opera about the legendary Spanish libertine Don Giovanni, who murders the Commendatore in a duel, seduces multiple women, and is ultimately dragged to hell by the Commendatore's stone statue when he refuses to repent. Libretto by Lorenzo Da Ponte. Full title: Il dissoluto punito, ossia il Don Giovanni (The Rake Punished, or Don Giovanni). Premiered in Prague.

works.name: Don Giovanni – The Work as a Whole
works.description: Mozart's opera in two acts, libretto by Lorenzo Da Ponte. The title libertine (Don Giovanni) kills the Commendatore in a duel at the opera's opening, and is ultimately dragged to hell by the Commendatore's stone statue when he refuses to repent. Full Italian title: Il dissoluto punito, ossia il Don Giovanni. Premiered in Prague in 1787. Eberhard Wächter played the title role in a famous 1959 recording conducted by Carlo Maria Giulini. Gustav Mahler cut the opera's final sextet when he conducted it. Pauline Viardot bought the original manuscript and built a shrine to it. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Title character is dragged to hell by a statue of the Commendatore (Donna Anna's father, whom he murdered) when he refuses to repent
examples: a statue of the Commendatore drags the title character of what Mozart opera to hell?
a feast of vipers and scorpions set on the cover of a tomb; the title libertine is dragged to hell by the Commendatore

works.name: The Catalogue Aria ("Madamina, il catalogo è questo")
works.description: Aria sung by Leporello (Don Giovanni's servant) listing his master's sexual conquests. Claims 1,003 Spanish women among the seductions. Leporello also punctuates the opening aria 'Notte e giorno faticar' with 'No no no no no.' Wikipedia

works.name: "La ci darem la mano" (Duet – Don Giovanni and Zerlina)
works.description: Duet between the title character and Zerlina (a peasant girl), in which he attempts to seduce her away from her fiancé Masetto. The aria opens with an ascending A, A, B, C-sharp, A, F-sharp, up to B motif. Chopin's set of variations on it prompted Robert Schumann to exclaim 'Hats off, gentlemen – a genius!' Wikipedia

works.name: The Commendatore / Final Scene
works.description: The climactic final scene of Act II: the stone statue of the murdered Commendatore comes to dinner, chants 'Pentiti!' (Repent!), and drags Don Giovanni to hell when he refuses. Leporello hides under the table. The scene is foreshadowed by syncopated D minor chords from the overture, which return as the statue knocks three times. Wikipedia

clues.clue: The statue chants 'Pentiti!' (Repent!) and Don Giovanni repeatedly responds 'No!' on a high D; he is dragged to hell
examples: In this opera's final scene, the title character responds 'no!' on a high D when ordered to 'repent' by the Commendatore

clues.clue: The Commendatore's three knocks are accompanied by loud D minor chords from the overture; the statue tells him 'a cenar teco m'invitasti' ('you invited me to dine with you')
examples: This opera's title character is told 'a cenar teco' by a character whose three knocks are accompanied by loud D minor chords from the overture

clues.clue: Don Giovanni had invited the statue to dinner; Leporello was too scared to do so, so the title character issued the invitation himself

works.name: The Overture
works.description: Begins in D minor with a loud tutti chord, followed by crashing syncopated chords in the violins and violas. Flutes and violins play an ascending and descending D minor scale, then eerily ascending major and melodic minor scales. The overture transitions without pause into the opening aria 'Notte e giorno faticar,' sung by Leporello. Imitates Gluck's Alceste. The opening chords are reharmonized into diminished seventh chords for the final scene. Wikipedia

clues.clue: D minor overture begins with a loud tutti chord followed by crashing syncopated chords in violins and violas; imitates the opening of Gluck's Alceste

works.name: The Ball Scene / Three Simultaneous Dances
works.description: During the masked ball at the end of Act I, the pit orchestra and two onstage ensembles play three dances simultaneously in different time signatures: a minuet in 3/4, a contradance (or 'follia') in 2/4, and a waltz (or 'teitsch') in 3/8. Wye Jamison Allanbrook's analysis of the dances and social class influenced Peter Sellars's 1988 Trump Tower production.

clues.clue: Three simultaneous dances at the ball: a minuet in 3/4, a contradance in 2/4, and a waltz in 3/8

works.name: The Feast Scene / Quotes from Other Operas
works.description: During Act II's feast scene, a diegetic onstage band plays excerpts from other popular operas (Una cosa rara by Martín y Soler, Fra i due litiganti by Sarti), culminating in 'Non più andrai' from Mozart's own Marriage of Figaro, to which Don Giovanni remarks 'I know this only too well!' Leporello is annoyed by the band.

works.description: Don Giovanni's comic servant/valet. Sings the opening aria 'Notte e giorno faticar' (Night and day I toil), in which he complains about his lot while standing guard. Also sings the Catalogue Aria. Punctuates the opening aria with 'No no no no no.' Hides under the table in the finale. Originated by bass Felice Ponziani. Wikipedia

works.name: Don Giovanni – Connections to Other Topics
works.description: The Don Juan legend originated in Tirso de Molina's play The Trickster of Seville and the Stone Guest. Later treatments include Molière's Dom Juan, Pushkin's 'little tragedy' The Stone Guest, Zorrilla's Don Juan Tenorio (performed every All Saints' Day), and Max Frisch's Don Juan, or The Love of Geometry. Byron's Don Juan is an unfinished comic epic in ottava rima. Kierkegaard analyzed the character in Either/Or as 'absolutely musical.'

clues.clue: Tirso de Molina's The Trickster of Seville and the Stone Guest is the first dramatic treatment of the Don Juan legend
examples: The first dramatization of this figure is Tirso de Molina's play The Trickster of Seville and the Stone Guest
Tirso de Molina created the first adaptation of a story in which this character foolishly invites the dead Commander to dinner

clues.clue: Byron wrote an epic poem (Don Juan) about the character; the protagonist beds women in a Turkish harem and earns Catherine the Great's favor

comprehensive_summary:
Don Giovanni (full title: Il dissoluto punito, ossia il Don Giovanni, 'The Rake Punished, or Don Giovanni') is a two-act opera by Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart with a libretto by Lorenzo Da Ponte, premiered in Prague in 1787. It stands as one of the central operas in the repertoire and a touchstone of Western music.

The opera follows the Spanish libertine Don Giovanni, who murders the Commendatore (Donna Anna's father) in a duel at the opera's opening, then spends the work seducing and deceiving various women. His servant Leporello opens the opera with the complaint 'Notte e giorno faticar' ('Night and day I toil') sung to quarter notes alternating between F and C. In the Catalogue Aria ('Madamina, il catalogo è questo'), Leporello enumerates his master's conquests for Donna Elvira, claiming 1,003 women in Spain alone and listing seductions by social class. The German (Viennese) version of the opera also includes Elvira herself in the list of Spanish conquests; the aria interpolates dotted arpeggiated D major figures between repetitions of 'mille e tre' ('one thousand and three').

The central Act I centerpiece is the duet 'La ci darem la mano,' sung between Don Giovanni and the peasant girl Zerlina as he attempts to seduce her away from her fiancé Masetto. The duet opens with an ascending motif (A, A, B, C-sharp, A, F-sharp, up to B) and shifts abruptly from 2/4 to 6/8 time when the two sing 'Andiam!' together. A teenage Frédéric Chopin composed a set of variations on this duet, prompting Robert Schumann's celebrated exclamation 'Hats off, gentlemen — a genius!' The Act I finale features one of the most complex scenes in opera: a masked ball at which the pit orchestra and two onstage ensembles simultaneously play three dances in different meters — a minuet in 3/4, a contradance in 2/4, and a waltz in 3/8. Musicologist Wye Jamison Allanbrook analyzed these dance rhythms in relation to social class, influencing Peter Sellars's 1988 Trump Tower production.

The overture opens in D minor with a loud tutti chord followed by crashing syncopated chords in the violins and violas — an effect that imitates the opening of Gluck's Alceste. Flutes and violins then play ascending and descending D minor scales, followed by eerily ascending major and melodic minor scales. The overture transitions without pause into Leporello's opening aria. Alfred Einstein posited that two recitatives in the opera were forgeries by Guardasoni. The D minor chords of the overture are reharmonized into diminished seventh chords for the opening of the final scene.

In the Act II feast scene, a diegetic onstage band plays excerpts from other operas — Una cosa rara (Martín y Soler) and Fra i due litiganti (Sarti) — before culminating in 'Non più andrai' from Mozart's own Marriage of Figaro. Don Giovanni remarks 'I know this only too well!', and Leporello is annoyed by the familiar tune. The climactic finale arrives when the stone statue of the murdered Commendatore comes to dinner, chanting 'Pentiti!' (Repent!) and grabbing Don Giovanni's hand. Don Giovanni repeatedly shouts 'No!' — on a high D — and is dragged to hell. At the premiere, this scene was rearranged so that the bass Giuseppe Lolli could change costumes to play a different character in the epilogue sextet. Leporello hides under the table during the confrontation. The final sextet, which Gustav Mahler was known to cut, closes with the moral 'this is how all evildoers end.'

Notable performers and productions: Eberhard Wächter played the title role in a famous 1959 recording conducted by Carlo Maria Giulini. Alfred Roller designed a white urn-embroidered jacket for a Mahler-era production. Pauline Viardot purchased the original manuscript and built a shrine to it; Tchaikovsky saw the score at her home and subsequently composed his Orchestral Suite No. 4 (Mozartiana). Lorenzo Da Ponte produced the Garcia family's 1825 New York tour, which staged this opera with Manuel Garcia Sr. as the title character, Manuel Jr. as Leporello, and Maria (Malibran) as Zerlina.

Philosopher Søren Kierkegaard devoted a long essay ('The Immediate Stages of the Erotic, or the Musical Erotic') in Either/Or to the analysis of Don Giovanni, arguing that the title character is 'not character but essentially life' and 'absolutely musical,' with his passion setting the passions of all others in motion. Don Giovanni is 'heard through' Leporello in the passage describing seasonal preferences among the conquests. The Don Juan legend itself originated in Tirso de Molina's The Trickster of Seville and the Stone Guest; subsequent treatments include Molière's Dom Juan, Pushkin's 'little tragedy' The Stone Guest, Zorrilla's Don Juan Tenorio, and Lord Byron's unfinished epic Don Juan in ottava rima.

recursive_suggestions: The Marriage of Figaro — frequently cross-referenced; feast scene quotes 'Non più andrai' from it; same Da Ponte trilogy
Lorenzo Da Ponte — librettist of Don Giovanni; mentioned as producing Garcia New York tour at Columbia
Søren Kierkegaard — extended analysis of Don Giovanni in Either/Or ('The Immediate Stages of the Erotic') is a recurring clue
